notes: By way of justification for discussing these matters, I remind you that they are reported with such frequency in the news media that one is constrained to join with the psalmist in his prayer, “Deliver my soul, O Lord, from lying lips, and from a deceitful tongue.” (Ps. 120:2 [Psalm 120:2].) (Marion G. Romney, in Conference Report, Oct. 1976, 50; or Ensign, Nov. 1976, 36)

quotes.text: By way of justification for discussing these matters, I remind you that they are reported with such frequency in the news media that one is constrained to join with the psalmist in his prayer, “Deliver my soul, O Lord, from lying lips, and from a deceitful tongue.” (Ps. 120:2 [Psalm 120:2].)
quotes.attr: Marion G. Romney, in Conference Report, Oct. 1976, 50; or Ensign, Nov. 1976, 36

notes: This "song of ascent" is a prayer for exaltation above worldly dangers. The "hills, from whence cometh my help" are the mountains of the Lord's house; thus it is made clear that "my help cometh from the Lord" (Ps. 121:1-2). This psalm, which is almost as well known as Psalm 23, "The Lord is my shepherd," gives the same assurance of protection in the walks of life but in a different idiom. It is another beautiful song. (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, p.460)
